text:
It has been a decade since the EU is in a permanent state of crisis management. The European Union's crisis governance is currently under the magnifying glass when it comes to democratic legitimacy. In particular, EU insiders and scholars argue that in Brussels there is a "state of exception" where the political elite expediently uses the urgency and exceptional circumstances of the EU's crisis situation to assert their advantage and maintain an asymmetrical power relationship that marginalizes citizens in the EU policy process and that may result in growing Euroscepticism. So far, however, there is no detailed empirical investigation into whether citizens actually feel deprived of their political rights and excluded in the technocratic and speedy EU governance style.

There are few real‐life crisis situations in which a particular set of emergency traits—such as high salience, synchronicity, and a certain degree of asymmetric or symmetric (structural) symmetry—can be implemented in a nearly identical manner across an entire continent such as Europe. The COVID‐19 pandemic offers, for the first time in history, a controlled real‐life experiment for empirically assessing how European societies react to this specific configuration of emergency traits. Building on emergency politics theory and connecting it to transnational cleavages, our research assesses how citizens in 15 EU member states evaluated the European Union's response to the COVID‐19 crisis.

It appears that citizens do not view emergency rule in the same way. Our results show that public opinions of emergency governance are largely mediated by prior attitudes towards the European Union and democracy. The transnational cleavage is thus still the underlying factor in Euroscepticism, while emergency governance only leads to protests over particular issues at the local level, without turning into a general legitimacy crisis. Overall, our findings suggest that the potential negative effects of crisis governance on public opinion are more limited than assumed in the theoretical literature. In fact, prior political loyalties are generally strong enough to counterbalance the potential disruptive effects of emergency measures.
